core_takeaway: Agent_1 did not gain a first-position payoff advantage. The group converged on preserving each agent's top item, and Agent_3's Jewel-only outcome beat Agent_1's Pencil-plus-Stone outcome because Jewel was worth more to Agent_3 than Pencil plus Stone was to Agent_1.
why_or_counterexample: This is a counterexample to first-position advantage: Agent_1 opened by aiming for Pencil and ideally Quill, but the accepted Round 2 baseline gave Agent_1 only Pencil and Stone while giving Agent_3 Jewel. Agent_3 used Jewel as a hard acceptance condition, and the reflection/private-voting record shows the table learned that consensus required Jewel with Agent_3. With the Round 2 discount applied, Agent_1 finished at 50.4 and Agent_3 at 57.6.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1's public first move anchored on Pencil and possible Quill, but public discussion shifted toward a norm of each agent receiving their top item: Pencil to Agent_1, Quill to Agent_2, Jewel to Agent_3. Because Agent_3 valued Jewel at 64, that fairness norm favored the last-position agent over Agent_1's final Pencil-plus-Stone bundle.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Private thinking is available in 6 entries. Across both rounds, agents privately identified the viable path as top-priority preservation with Apple and Stone as balancing tokens; Agent_1's private plans accepted small-item concessions rather than converting first position into a dominant bundle.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Round 1 formal proposals omitted Agent_3 from Jewel and failed unanimously. Round 2 proposals by Agent_1 and Agent_3 converged on the same accepted allocation: Agent_1 gets Pencil and Stone, Agent_2 gets Apple and Quill, and Agent_3 gets Jewel.
phase_notes.private_voting: Private voting is available in 18 entries. Round 1 votes show Agent_3 rejected every proposal that lacked Jewel; Round 2 votes show all agents accepted the top-priority baseline and rejected Agent_2's over-concentrated alternative.
phase_notes.reflection: Reflection is available in 3 entries. Reflections explicitly diagnosed Jewel-to-Agent_3 as the consensus blocker and recommended the Round 2 baseline that ultimately made Agent_3, not Agent_1, the highest utility recipient.

mechanisms.theme: Final arithmetic favors the last position
mechanisms.explanation: The final allocation gave Agent_1 Pencil plus Stone while Agent_3 received Jewel. Since Agent_3 valued Jewel at 64 and Agent_1 valued Pencil plus Stone at 56, the accepted fairness baseline made the last-position agent the payoff winner after discounting.

evidence.quote: - final_utilities: {'Agent_1': 50.4, 'Agent_2': 45.9, 'Agent_3': 57.6}
- final_allocation: {'Agent_1': [4, 2], 'Agent_2': [0, 3], 'Agent_3': [1]}

mechanisms.theme: Agent_1's opening anchor did not bind the outcome
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 initially sought the strongest first-position bundle, including Pencil and Quill, and even proposed taking both in Round 1. That bid failed; by Round 2 Agent_1 accepted Pencil and Stone as the consensus-preserving bundle.

evidence.quote: secure Pencil, ideally along with Quill
I would only get Pencil+Stone (56), which is far below Proposal 1
Proposal 1 gives me Pencil and Stone

mechanisms.theme: Agent_3's Jewel demand operated as a veto point
mechanisms.explanation: The last speaker made Jewel central early, and private voting shows that proposals denying Jewel to Agent_3 had no path to unanimity. This gave Agent_3 a stronger constraint on the final deal than Agent_1's first-mover proposal.

evidence.quote: Jewel is my top item (by a large margin)
Jewel is my top priority
I need Jewel allocated to me (Agent_3)
Jewel distribution is the sticking point.

mechanisms.theme: Top-priority fairness became the shared consensus norm
mechanisms.explanation: Public discussion, private thinking, private voting, and reflection all converged on the same rule: preserve Pencil, Quill, and Jewel with their main claimants while using Apple and Stone as minor balancing tokens. That rule helped Agent_3 because their preserved top item was the highest-valued final item.

evidence.quote: Keeps all three top items with their preferred owners
core priorities are aligned around preserving Pencil with Agent_1, Quill with Agent_2, and Jewel with Agent_3
Jewel with Agent_3, Quill with Agent_2, Pencil with Agent_1
Respects top priorities: Jewel with Agent_3, Quill with Agent_2, Pencil with Agent_1.

mechanisms.theme: Formal Round 2 voting ratified the counterexample
mechanisms.explanation: The accepted Round 2 proposals by Agent_1 and Agent_3 were substantively identical. The table unanimously accepted that baseline and rejected the alternative that would have deprived Agent_3 of Jewel, confirming that consensus, not first position, controlled the result.

depriving Agent_3 of their top item and risking lack of consensus
